title: C# Basic — Paper 7
description: C# concepts: switch expressions/patterns, loops, loop control, arrays, variables, enums, access modifiers, properties, static members, and inheritance basics.

questions.text: What is a tuple pattern in a C# switch expression?

options.text: A pattern that matches against multiple values simultaneously using tuple syntax
A pattern that matches a type that inherits from Tuple
A pattern that only works with named tuples
A pattern that matches elements of an array

questions.text: What does the `and` pattern do in a C# switch expression?

options.text: Matches when either of two sub-patterns match
Matches when both sub-patterns match simultaneously
Negates a pattern
Combines two types in a single check

questions.text: What is a positional pattern in C# pattern matching?

options.text: A pattern that matches based on the position of a type in an inheritance hierarchy
A pattern that uses deconstruction to match properties positionally
A pattern that matches based on array index
A pattern that uses named arguments

questions.text: What is the output of `for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) { if (i == 3) break; Console.Write(i); }`?
What does `continue` print if used inside `for (int i=0; i<5; i++) { if (i%2==0) continue; Console.Write(i); }`?
Why is `goto` generally discouraged in modern C# code?

options.text: It is not supported in C# 8+
It can make code flow hard to follow and maintain (spaghetti code)
It causes memory leaks
It is slower than structured loops

questions.text: What does `Array.Length` return for a 2D array `int[3,4]` in C#?
How do you access the element at row 2, column 1 of a 2D array `arr` in C#?

options.text: arr.Get(2,1)

questions.text: What method copies elements from one array to another in C#?

options.text: Array.Move()
Array.Clone()
Array.Copy()
Array.Transfer()

questions.text: Which of the following correctly uses the `var` keyword in C#?

options.text: var x; x = 10;
var x = 10;
var int x = 10;
var x = null;

questions.text: What is the difference between `const` and `readonly` in C#?

options.text: `const` can be set at runtime; `readonly` must be set at compile time
`const` is a compile-time constant; `readonly` can be set in the constructor at runtime
They are identical in behavior
`readonly` cannot be used in classes, only in structs

questions.text: What are the ranges of `int` and `uint` in C#?

options.text: Both are 0 to 2^32-1

questions.text: How do you cast an `int` to an enum named `Status` in C#?

options.text: Status s = Status.Parse(1);
Status s = (Status)1;
Status s = Status[1];
Status s = Enum.Convert(1);

questions.text: What does `Enum.Parse(typeof(Direction), "North")` do in C#?

options.text: Returns the integer value of North in the enum
Returns the Direction enum member corresponding to the string "North"
Throws an exception always
Creates a new enum member called "North"

questions.text: How do you define an enum with `byte` as the underlying type in C#?

options.text: byte enum Color { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Color as byte { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Color : byte { Red, Green, Blue }
enum Color { Red=byte, Green=byte, Blue=byte }

questions.text: What is the `protected` access modifier's effect in C# when used on a method?

options.text: The method can only be called from within the same file
The method is accessible within the declaring class and all derived classes
The method is only accessible within the same assembly
The method is inaccessible from derived classes outside the assembly

questions.text: What happens when you try to access a `private` member of a base class from a derived class in C#?

options.text: It is accessible because the derived class inherits all members
A compile-time error occurs because private members are not accessible in derived classes
It is accessible if the derived class is in the same file
A runtime exception is thrown

questions.text: What is the default access level for a top-level class in C# (not nested)?

options.text: public
private
internal
protected

questions.text: What does `public int Count { get; set; } = 0;` declare in C#?

options.text: A read-only property defaulting to 0
An auto-implemented property with a default initial value of 0
A static property always returning 0
A field named Count

questions.text: What is the difference between `public string Name { get; set; }` and `public string Name { get; }`?

options.text: Both are mutable
The first allows reading and writing; the second is read-only
The second allows writing but not reading
They are identical

questions.text: How do you declare a computed (expression-bodied) property that returns a full name from FirstName and LastName?

options.text: public string FullName { return FirstName + " " + LastName; }
public string FullName => FirstName + " " + LastName;
public string FullName { get { yield return FirstName; } }
public string FullName = FirstName + LastName;

questions.text: What keyword can you use to call a base class's static method from a derived class in C#?

options.text: base.MethodName()
BaseClassName.MethodName()
super.MethodName()
parent.MethodName()

questions.text: What is a static property in C# and how is it accessed?

options.text: A property that can only be read, accessed via an instance
A property belonging to the class, accessed via the class name without an instance
A property that cannot be changed once set
A property shared between two classes

questions.text: What is an abstract method in C#?

options.text: A method with no return type
A method declared without a body in an abstract class that derived classes must implement
A static method that cannot be overridden
A private method in an interface

questions.text: What does the `base` keyword do in a derived class constructor in C#?

options.text: It calls a static method of the base class
It calls the constructor of the base class
It refers to the current instance
It prevents the base class from being instantiated

questions.text: What does the `is` keyword return when checking `myObj is MyClass c` in C#?

options.text: An integer indicating type compatibility
A bool: true if myObj is a MyClass, and assigns it to c; false otherwise
A new instance of MyClass
The type of myObj

questions.text: What is the `as` operator used for in C# and what does it return on failure?

options.text: Converts a type; throws InvalidCastException on failure
Checks type equality; returns 0 on failure
Attempts a reference or nullable conversion; returns null on failure
Performs an explicit cast; throws NullReferenceException on failure

questions.text: What does a `sealed` method (inside a non-sealed class) prevent in C#?

options.text: The method from being called by anyone
Further overriding of that method in classes derived from this class
The method from being static
The class from being inherited

questions.text: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface in C#?

options.text: An abstract class can have implemented methods and state; an interface (pre-C#8) only declares members without implementation
Both can only declare members without implementation
An interface can have constructors; an abstract class cannot
A class can implement multiple abstract classes but only one interface

questions.text: Can a class implement multiple interfaces in C#?

options.text: No, only one interface is allowed
Yes, a class can implement multiple interfaces
Only if the interfaces share the same base interface
Only if the class is abstract
